preamble: The transformation from a voiceless palatal plosive [c] to a voiceless postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is a classic case of affrication. This often occurs during the process of palatalization, where a plosive develops a fricated release as it moves toward the palate.

examples.note: The Latin [k] before front vowels became palatalized to [c] and subsequently affricated to [tʃ] in Italian.
The Proto-Germanic *k underwent palatalization and affrication in Old English when preceding certain front vowels (e.g., *kīsiz → cīese).

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
